title: Rice pilaf
subtitle: Toasted basmati cooked in spiced stock until each grain is separate and fluffy
excerpt: Basmati rice toasted in butter until fragrant, then cooked in spiced chicken stock with cinnamon and allspice until the liquid is absorbed and every grain is separate. The pilaf method (toast, add stock, cook undisturbed) produces a cleaner, more aromatic result than boiling and draining.
sourceNotes: The pilaf method is documented in Ottoman and Persian cookery manuscripts from the medieval period. The technique of toasting rice in fat before cooking in stock is described in the fourteenth-century Persian cookbook Nuskhah-i Shahjahani and spread widely through Ottoman, Middle Eastern, and Central Asian cooking. The word pilaf derives from the Ottoman Turkish pilav.

recipe.batchNotes: Makes excellent leftovers. Reheat in a covered pan with a splash of water.

content.text: The key to pilaf is what happens before the liquid is added. The rice is toasted in butter over medium heat until it smells nutty and the grains begin to turn opaque — about 3 minutes. This coats the outside of each grain and slows water absorption, which is why pilaf grains stay separate rather than clumping together. Rinse the rice before toasting to remove excess starch.

The ratio is 1 part rice to 1.7 parts stock. Do not add more liquid if the rice looks dry mid-cook — lift the lid as little as possible. The 10-minute rest off the heat finishes the cooking and lets the steam settle. Fluff with a fork, not a spoon, which would compact the grains.
Soften the onion and toast the rice

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a heavy-based saucepan with a tight-fitting lid over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 to 8 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, and {{allspice}} of allspice. Stir for 30 seconds. Add the rinsed {{basmati-rice}} of rice and stir for 3 minutes until the grains turn opaque and smell faintly nutty.

Pour in the hot {{stock-chicken}} of stock and add the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir once to distribute the rice, then bring to the boil. Reduce the heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly, and cook for 15 minutes without lifting the lid. Remove from the heat and rest, covered, for 10 minutes. Remove the bay leaves, fluff the rice with a fork, and serve.

items.cause: Not rinsed before cooking, or too much liquid
items.fix: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear before cooking; check the stock ratio is 1:1.7 rice to liquid

items.cause: Lid not tight-fitting, or heat not low enough
items.fix: Add 2 tablespoons of boiling water, cover, and cook for 5 more minutes; the steam is what finishes the top layer of rice

items.symptom: Grains are hard at the top but cooked below
items.cause: Resting step skipped or lid removed too soon
items.fix: The 10-minute rest is not optional — the steam settles and finishes the grains at the top; keep the lid on

content.text: The pilaf method (toasting grains in fat, then cooking in a measured quantity of liquid) originated in Persia and the Ottoman Empire and spread across a vast geographical range, from India (where it became pulao and biryani) to Central Asia (plov), across the Middle East and into Southern Europe. Turkish pilav, Persian chelo, Indian pulao, and Spanish paella are all variations on the same technique. The principle is the same in each: fat coats the starch, the stock provides flavour, and the closed vessel traps steam that cooks the grains from above. Boiling and draining is simpler but produces a different result.
